EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very active frontal system brought heavy snow to the Northern Oregon Cascades Foothills on the 3rd with snow totals of 11 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event, starting late Sunday February 27th and continuing through Monday February 28th, brought heavy rain and strong winds to northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Rainfall amounts varied from around one inch in the Willamette Valley, to 5 to 10 inches in the Coast Range as well as the Washington and northern Oregon Cascades. Widespread urban and small stream flooding occurred in the Greater Portland-Metro area, and rivers flooded along the coast, in particular the Grays, Wilson and Nehalem rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yaquina Bridge measured a max wind of 49 mph with gust of 63 mph.

In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were reported along the central Oregon coast with maximum sustained winds between 35 and 75 mph. A peak gust of 86 mph occurred on the Yaquina Bay Bridge (ODT38).  There was a downed tree and powerlines over highway 229 near Kernville at 2300PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of northwest flow over the Pacific Northwest guided several rounds of moisture advection across the mountain zones, leading to upslope moderate to heavy snow showers nearly each day. Meanwhile, surface frontal systems and a series of shortwave troughs in the flow aloft produced moderate to heavy snow as well as some periods of freezing rain across the lower elevations. Strong winds were also produced as a result of these shortwave troughs and surface frontal systems at the beginning and end of the prolonged winter weather conditions. During this extended period of winter weather, the I-84 and several state and US highways in Oregon and Washington closed for extended periods of time due to increased traffic accidents from ice and/or moderate to heavy snow accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station KLGD measured a peak wind gust of 63 MPH at 0748 PST on Jan. 2nd. The mesonet station DW6879 1 mile south of Union, OR measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph at 0800 PST on Jan. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eruption of Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai in Tonga on January 14th at 2027PST generated a tsunami that reach much of the Pacific rim, including the U.S. West coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tsunami generated by an eruption of the Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'api volcano reached the U.S. West coast.  A tsunami wave height of 0.2 feet was measured at the Astoria tide gauge on the 15th. No reports of damage or injury are known at this time.

This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were reported in the Coast Range of Northwest Oregon. Mt. Hebo (AR658) had a maximum sustained wind of 38 mph with a peak gust to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of northwest flow over the Pacific Northwest guided several rounds of moisture advection across the mountain zones, leading to upslope moderate to heavy snow showers nearly each day. Meanwhile, surface frontal systems and a series of shortwave troughs in the flow aloft produced moderate to heavy snow as well as some periods of freezing rain across the lower elevations. Strong winds were also produced as a result of these shortwave troughs and surface frontal systems at the beginning and end of the prolonged winter weather conditions. During this extended period of winter weather, the I-84 and several state and US highways in Oregon and Washington closed for extended periods of time due to increased traffic accidents from ice and/or moderate to heavy snow accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon DOT station ODT43 5 miles WSW of Shaniko, OR measured a 69 mph wind gust at 0515 PST on Jan. 3rd. The North Pole Ridge RAWS station measured a 68 mph wing gust at 0248 PST on Jan. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eruption of Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai in Tonga on January 14th at 2027PST generated a tsunami that reach much of the Pacific rim, including the U.S. West coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tsunami generated by an eruption of the Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'api volcano reached the U.S. West.  A tsunami wave height of 0.7 feet was measured at the Newport tide gauge on the 15th. No reports of damage or injury are known at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very active frontal system brought heavy snow to the Northern Oregon Cascades on the 2nd and 3rd with snow totals mostly between 21 and 29 inches. Peak snow fall rates are estimated at 3 to 4 inches per hour. One particular site, Bear Grass SNOTEL (BKSO3) at 4720 feet had 34 inches snowfall. Another site, Red Hill SNOTEL (REDO3) at 4410 feet received 18 inches of snow in 6 hours, on the 3rd between 0200PST to 0800PST. US-20 over Santiam Pass was closed due to heavy snow.

In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were reported along the north Oregon coast with sustained winds between 45 and 55 mph with a peak gust to 77 mph at Garibaldi (GARO3.)  Around 2030PST tree branches were reported by ODOT over highway 26 near Necanicum resulting in traffic delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough lifting northeast across the region produced isolated thunderstorms across northeast Oregon in the afternoon of May 15th. At least one of these thunderstorms was strong enough to produce severe hail as it crossed I-84 several miles east of Pendleton, Or.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report 4 miles north northwest of Meacham, OR reported a thunderstorm that was producing 1.5 inch hail stones near exit 234 along I-84 in northeast OR at 1540 PST. A trained spotter 6 miles south southwest of Thorn Hollow, OR also reported hail that ranged in size from 0.75 to 1 inches and accumulated up to 1.5 inches deep at 1540 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper low pressure systems moving across northeast OR in late May produced several rounds of heavy rain in the Blue Mountains and along the foothill locations. Several reports of water over local roadways, washouts of gravel roads, and heavy rain were reported during the day on May 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 9 miles west of Meacham, OR reported water covering county roadways making them impassable at 0900 PST on May 29th. A public Facebook report from 8 miles east northeast of Pilot Rock, OR had video of flooding over Popcorn Ln along Mckay Creek, making the road impassable at 0900 PST on May 29th. A NWS Employee reporting 4 miles south of Mission, OR reported water covering the intersection of Lloyd Rd. and Market Rd due to flooding along the Pattawa Creek at 1013 PST on May 29th. Another report 4 miles east southeast of Pilot Rock, OR reported 2.06 inches of rainfall over an 18 hour period, with water over Stewart Creek Rd and other local roads at  PST on May 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed several low pressure systems across the Pacific Northwest in the middle of April.  The trough allowed for a much colder than normal air mass to settle into portions of eastern Washington and Oregon, allowing snow levels to drop to the surface across a majority of these areas. This ultimately resulted in snow accumulations in the mountains and lower elevations, with significant snow accumulations being observed in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Starr Ridge SNOTEL 11 miles south southwest of John Day, OR measured 7 inches of new in a 24 hour period ending at 1500 PDT. The Ochoco Meadows SNOTEL 13 miles southwest of Mitchell, OR measured 8 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 2300 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of rain combined with snowmelt caused flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage (21.0 feet) at 04/1215 PST. The river crested just above moderate flood stage (23.0 feet) at 23.01 feet at 05/2245 PST.  This one reading was the only one that exceeded moderate flood stage. The river dropped below minor flood stage at 07/2230 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass combined with clearing skies to make a cold morning across southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 32 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed several low pressure systems across the Pacific Northwest in the middle of April.  The trough allowed for a much colder than normal air mass to settle into portions of eastern Washington and Oregon, allowing snow levels to drop to the surface across a majority of these areas. This ultimately resulted in snow accumulations in the mountains and lower elevations, with significant snow accumulations being observed in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The milk Shakes SNOTEL 9 miles southwest of Ski Bluewood, WA measured 12 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0400 PDT. A report relayed from a media partner estimated that 14 inches of new snow was measured at 0622 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed several low pressure systems across the Pacific Northwest in the middle of April.  The trough allowed for a much colder than normal air mass to settle into portions of eastern Washington and Oregon, allowing snow levels to drop to the surface across a majority of these areas. This ultimately resulted in snow accumulations in the mountains and lower elevations, with significant snow accumulations being observed in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Dale, OR the Oregon Dept. of Highways measured 9 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0104 PDT. The Oregon Dept. of Highways measured 9 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0120 PDT 8 miles north northwest of Ukiah, OR.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level shortwave trough coupled with a cold front passage at the surface provided showery activity through most of the day. During the afternoon, these systems interacted with increasing instability, strong bulk shear, and upsloping into mountain areas allowed some of these showers to strengthen into strong thunderstorms that produced severe hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports about 10 miles south of Pendleton, OR reported a thunderstorm that was producing hail between the sizes of 1 inch to 1.25 inches between 1700 PST and 1730 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed offshore and moved across the central Willamette Valley, bringing windy conditions and some wind damage to the south and central Willamette valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 46 mph at Mahlon Sweet Field (KEUG) at 1353 PST. Gusty winds developed during the afternoon with gusts mostly in the 25-35 mph range. Winds subsided in the evening. Eugene Public Utility District reported about 1600 customers without electrical power in the Pleasant Hill, Dexter, Cottage Grove and Cloverdale areas. A couple reports of trees blown over in Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed offshore and moved across the central Willamette Valley, bringing windy conditions and some wind damage to the south and central Willamette valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported Maple tree blown down in neighborhood about 2 miles east of Salem Airport. The ASOS at Salem Airport (KSLE) reported a gust to 41 mph around that time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from an isolated thunderstorm struck a tree, destroying it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clackamas County Sheriff reported a call of lighting destroying a large tree near Canby, Oregon. Location of event is a guess.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level shortwave trough coupled with a cold front passage at the surface provided showery activity through most of the day. During the afternoon, these systems interacted with increasing instability, strong bulk shear, and upsloping into mountain areas allowed some of these showers to strengthen into strong thunderstorms that produced severe hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1244 PST, a tornado spun up 7 miles east southeast of Weston, OR along OR Highway 204 and moved east crossing OR Highway 204 3 times before veering left about 1.9 miles from it's start point. The tornado continued east north east for 0.80 miles across a draw north of OR Hwy 204, following parallel to Lower Basket Mountain Rd for about 0.8 miles before dissipating. Many trees were either uprooted or snapped while covering the highway and other roads. Several residences saw roof damage, including failure and uplifting of a partial roof that was transported 300 feet. A farm outbuilding was also heavily damaged where the roof and walls had collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air was in place over the Pacific Northwest, with temperatures marginally cold enough for a low elevation snow event from the Portland metro northward.  A storm system moved over northwest Oregon and southwest Washington bringing widespread snowfall to many areas north of Salem, Oregon. This system brought several inches of snow to the Willamette Valley in addition to the Coast Range and Cascades. By the late morning/early afternoon, much of the snow west of the Columbia River Gorge had transitioned to rain. An impressive low pressure system near 995 mb developed offshore and moved straight eastward with a track just south of the Portland metro area, which is often considered an ideal snow track in winter, not April.  The result was the latest measurable snowfall on record at both Portland Airport and Downtown Portland.  Records for Downtown Portland go back to the 19th century.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevations above 1500 feet received up to 6 to 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low coupled with cold front at the surface brought numerous wind and winter weather impacts across portions of eastern Washington and eastern Oregon. Moisture rounding the upper low produced heavy snow across portions of the east slopes of the Cascades, while the cold front produced strong winds in the lower elevations during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station FW6922 Hood River 7 miles northeast of Dufur, OR measured a wind gust of 59 mph at 1245 PDT. The RAWS station North Pole Ridge 11 miles east of Shaniko, OR measured a wind gust of 59 mph at 1148 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system brought high winds to the Oregon coast as well as across the Coast Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor at Garibaldi measured a max wind of 42 mph with a gust of 67 mph. The sensor at Clatsop Spit measured a max wind of 45 mph with a gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system brought high winds to the Oregon coast as well as across the Coast Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Yaquina Bridge, a max wind of 45 mph with a gust of 62 mph was measured. Florence also measured a peak gust of 61 mph, while Newport has a sustained wind of 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system brought high winds to the Oregon coast as well as across the Coast Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rockhouse RAWS measured a max wind of 30 mph with a gusts of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system brought high winds to the Oregon coast as well as across the Coast Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Can infer high winds occurred within the north Coast Range by reports from WAZ020 and ORZ004, where gusts to 65 mph were measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief gustnado appears to have formed near the edge of a strong shower, causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video on social media showed what appeared to be a thunderstorm gust or gustnado, which caused minor damage to baseball stands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers continued behind a strong Pacific frontal system, dropping around 12 to 24 inches of snow in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 12 to 24 inches of snow fell, much of it in a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate atmospheric river event associated with a cold front across the region produced heavy snow in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 10 to 20 inches of snow fell above roughly 3500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate atmospheric river event associated with a cold front across the region produced heavy snow in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 6 to 18 inches of snow fell above roughly 4000 feet. Heaviest snow was above 4500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to the parts of the Oregon coast and windy conditions to the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a large tree down in Eugene along Goodpasture Island Road. Eugene airport ASOS reported peak gust to 53 mph between 2000PST and 2100PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to the parts of the Oregon coast and windy conditions to the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forest Grove Fire department reported a tree and powerline down on southwest Dilly Road in Forest Grove. Wind report from Forest Grove agrimet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to the parts of the Oregon coast and windy conditions to the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yaquina Bridge sensor (ODT38) reported sustained winds of 47 mph gusting to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front in the region brought heavy rain and breezy conditions to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds brought down trees on powerlines causing power outage and blocked roads in southwest Portland. All lanes of OR 99W, SW Barbur Blvd, between Capitol Hwy and Hamilton St. were closed. Up to 1400 Portland General Electric customers were affected.  Gusty south winds to 40 mph were reported in the surrounding area with gust to 19 mph in the immediate area of the incident.  Antecedent rainfall may have contributed to the downed trees as there was 1.66 inches of rain at the Portland Airport in the previous day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a large tree to fall, bringing down power lines across Highway 30 near the St. Johns Bridge, closing the highway in both directions for about 2 hours. The fallen tree also hit a house near the bridge. A daily record rainfall was recorded at Portland International Airport of 1.42 inches on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies and a cold air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing along parts of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 31 to 33 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to several locations in southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 12/1713 PST.  The ODOT sensor on US101 at Humbug Mountain recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 12/1616 PST and 12/1945 PST.  The peak gust was 68 mph recorded at 12/1945 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to several locations in southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor on US395 at Valley Falls recorded gust to 58 mph at 12/1404 PST and 12/1544 PST.  A CWOP at Summer Lake recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 13/0516 PST and 13/0845 PST.  The peak gust was 65 mph recorded at 13/0830 PST.  The Summer Lake RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 13/0538 PST and a gust to 58 mph at 13/0838 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass developed as an upper trough moved into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of funnel cloud about 1 mile north of Turner, Oregon. Location and track of funnel cloud estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a sewage overflow into the Willamette River in Milwaukie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calculated heat risk values at the Moro GHCN site recorded several days between July 25th through Aug. 1st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calculated heat risk values at the Bend and Redmond GHCN sites recorded several days between July 28th through the Aug. 1st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging and persistent surface high pressure in the Columbia Basin resulted in several areas reaching or exceeding critical heat risk thresholds during this period, with some mountain sites also seeing consecutive days of 95 degrees or greater temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roberts Butte RAWS and the Joseph Airport AWOS recorded consecutive maximum daily high temperatures of 95 degrees or greater between Aug. 17th and 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from an atmospheric river caused minor flooding and debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused debris flow covering HWY 224 about 5 miles from Ripplebrook.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure from the four corners region extended northward into the Pacific Northwest, which resulted in well above normal temperatures across the region for several days. Many stations saw temperatures exceed critical heat risk thresholds, while stations in some mountain zones saw high temperatures consecutively meet or exceed 95 degrees under the strengthening high pressure aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colgate RAWS recorded consecutive maximum daily temperatures of 95 degrees or greater on Aug. 8th and 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture and instability the night of Aug. 8th and on the 9th resulted in the development of strong thunderstorms capable of strong winds and heavy downpours across portions of central Oregon. These storms were in fact able to produce strong enough winds that brought down trees between Sunriver and La Pine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced wind gusts strong enough to down several trees, with several photos from the public showing trees snapped at the trunks, powerlines either toppled or snapped at the base, and damage to residential fencing from a snapped tree. Another public report also indicated that a tree had fallen onto a home in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed along the Northern  Blue Mountain Foothills in Oregon in the late afternoon and evening on Aug. 10th. The storms strengthened as they approached the state border with Washington, resulting in a few of the storms producing damaging winds as they passed over Milton-Freewater, OR. The strongest winds were estimated at 90mph, while damage to several homes, trees, and crops were observed during a storm damage survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey conducted the day after the event revealed areas of isolated down trees and tree branches, where one of the downed trees had landed on a home. There was also an area of corn crops that had been blown over in a unidirectional manner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed along the Northern  Blue Mountain Foothills in Oregon in the late afternoon and evening on Aug. 10th. The strongest winds were estimated at 90mph, while damage to several homes, trees, and crops were observed during a storm damage survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst occurred from a strong thunderstorm that affected several unoccupied, parked mobile homes. One of the mobile homes was lifted 30 feet into a neighboring mobile home, destroying the lifted mobile home. Other mobile homes suffered some roof and other structural damage, as evidenced from broken glass and insulation around the site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from an atmospheric river caused minor flooding and debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people and one dog were rescued Saturday after being reportedly stranded along Johnson Creek, which began flooding Friday night as an atmospheric river drenched the metro area. Also, portion of the Eastbank Esplanade closed due to rising of the Willamette River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough on the West Coast slowly edged in between a ridge of high pressure to the east of the region. This allowed for monsoon moisture to move into the region along with strong southwest flow. Several days of isolated supercells occurred, bringing heavy rain, localized flooding, and large hail to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter sent a message on social media confirming hail size seen on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough on the West Coast slowly edged in between a ridge of high pressure to the east of the region. This allowed for monsoon moisture to move into the region along with strong southwest flow. Several days of isolated supercells occurred, bringing heavy rain, localized flooding, and large hail to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station RTFO3 at Red Butte measured a severe wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that developed in southern Union county went on to impact the towns of La Grande and Wallowa in the afternoon on the 11th. Through the life cycle of the storm, it had produced hailstones to the size of 2.5 inches, while also producing winds up to 60mph. Hailstones produced by the storm caused damage to the sidings and windows of homes as well as produced dents in uncovered cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report from Facebook report from La Grande, OR indicated that hailstones larger than a quarter and up to the size of 1.5 inches had fallen around 2:50pm on the 11th. Further upstream, a trained spotter 1 mile southeast of Imbler, OR reported hailstones up to 1.25 inches. A measured wind gust to 60 mph occurred at the La Grande Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that developed in southern Union county went on to impact the towns of La Grande and Wallowa in the afternoon on the 11th. Through the life cycle of the storm, it had produced hailstones to the size of 2.5 inches, while also producing winds up to 60mph. Hailstones produced by the storm caused damage to the sidings and windows of homes as well as produced dents in uncovered cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Wallowa, OR reported a severe thunderstorm that was producing hailstones the size of tennis balls. Reports and pictures received from the public also indicated that hail had damaged the sidings of windows of homes, as well as damaging the panels and windows of uncovered vehicles. 4 injuries from the large hailstones were also relayed by the trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level high pressure over the Great Basin with a trough off the northern California coast brought unstable southerly flow over the region.  Several wildfire starts, including the Cedar Creek Fire, was attributed to thunderstorms that rolled across the area on the evening of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started an estimated 20 to 30 wildfires in the Willamette National Forest in early August.  The Cedar Creek Fire started on the morning of the 1st. The fire grew to around 7800 acres by the end of the month.  Impacts included smoke settling in the Oregon 58 corridor affecting the towns of Oakridge and Westfir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave developed as strong high pressure formed over the Pacific Northwest. This event was most notable by it's duration.  High temperatures around 100 degrees persisted for a week for much of the northwest Oregon and southwest Washington, except the coast.  Heat indicies ranged from 95 to 105 degrees during the afternoons. This resulted in several suspected deaths related to the heat.  The heatwave lasted through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Multnomah County Medical Examiner reported suspected deaths associated with the heat wave. One each on July 25th, 26th, 27th; two each on the 28th and 30th, all in Portland. A man drowned on the Sandy River on the 27th while paddle boarding. During the heatwave there were elevated numbers of heat related illnesses. For example on the 30th, Multnomah County reported 10 requests for Emergency/Urgent care. A typical summer day is zero to one. There was a cumulative of 63 heat illness, more than half of a typical summer. In another incident on July 25th, a man drowned while swimming at Henry Hagg Lake in Washington County.  Also in Washington County, a dog died from the heat when left in a car. Other suspected heat related deaths occurred July 28th and 29th in Clackamas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave developed as strong high pressure formed over the Pacific Northwest. This event was most notable by it's duration.  High temperatures around 100 degrees persisted for a week for much of the northwest Oregon and southwest Washington, except the coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four suspected heat related deaths occurred in Marion county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly advancing low pressure system off the coast and a ridging over the intermountain west allowed moisture in the mid-levels to advect into the central OR area. Increasing instability from hot temperatures allowed for a strong thunderstorm to develop near the the Madras, OR area in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madras airport ASOS measured a wind gust of 67mph at 5:55pm. An NWS employee 1 mile southwest of Madras, OR also witnessed thunderstorm outflow winds that blew down fences, produced low visibilities in blowing dust, and also produced a gustnado in nearby farm fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calculated heat risk values at the The Pendleton and Milton Freewater GHCN recorded consecutive days between July 27th through July 31st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river set up over the Pacific Northwest, directing heavy rains and strong wind to into portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over an approximately 2 year old burn area (Riverside Fire) triggered a debris flow. This is reportedly to have occurred sometime on November 5th. The debris flow crossed Highway 224 at the junction with Indian Henry Road, near a bridge crossing the Clackamas River. A nearby rain gauge had 3.31 inches in the 24 hours ending 1700PST November 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river set up over the Pacific Northwest, directing heavy rains and strong wind to into portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to recent heavy rain, wind gusts as high as 43 mph resulted in tree damage and power outages in the area. An estimated 26,000 customers had lost electrical power resulting in some school and business closures. According to Multnomah County Sheriff���s office, strong winds factored a floating home to break off it mooring from a Northeast Marine Drive marina along the Columbia River, eventually floating to Washington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines 6 miles east of Multnomah Falls on I-84, interstate was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds blew through the Western Columbia River Gorge as high pressure set up east of the Cascades, squeezing through the Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East wind gust to 94 mph recorded at Crown Point (DW6193), and to 78 mph at Corbett (CW2664) .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak EF-0 tornado occurred in rural Woodburn with minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado occurred in rural Woodburn, confirmed by video and supported by damage photos obtained from the public. Rated EF-0 with winds estimated between 61-74 mph. Damage to two residential properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought gusty winds and snow to south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summit RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 06/2203 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder airmass associated with a trough passage over the area infiltrated the John Day Basin, resulting in freezing temperatures the morning of November 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station OD200 US26 at Picture Gorge recorded temperatures at or below freezing between 0511 PST and 0721 PST. The mesonet station JHNO3 John Day River near John Day recorded temperatures at or below freezing between 0200 PST and 0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving across the region lowered snow levels to the surface across the eastern mountains, resulting in rain transitioning to snow in these areas. Heavier bands of snow across Wallowa County overnight Tuesday and early Wednesday morning resulted in 6 inches of snow accumulating across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 4 miles west of Joseph, OR measured 6 inches of new snowfall that ended before 0500 PST on November 2nd. A Cocorahs observer 1 mile southeast of Flora, OR measured 6 inches of new snowfall that ended before 0600 PST on November 2nd.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moving through the region and northwest flow aloft resulted in moderate to heavy snow accumulations across the mountains in northeast Oregon and the east slopes of the Cascades in Oregon between Nov. 27th and 28th. Snow amounts of 8 to 12 inches were reported in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report 30 miles W of Maupin, OR from the Oregon Dept. of Transportation indicated 12 inches of new snow in a 21 hour period ending at 0606 PST on Nov. 28th. The SNOTEL site Hogg Pass measured 10 inches of new snow between 0400 Nov. 27th and 0900 Nov. 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moving through the region and northwest flow aloft resulted in moderate to heavy snow accumulations across the mountains in northeast Oregon and the east slopes of the Cascades in Oregon between Nov. 27th and 28th. Snow amounts of 8 to 12 inches were reported in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site Beaver Reservoir measured 9 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1200 PST on Nov. 28th. The SNOTEL site County Line measured 8 inches of new snow in a 13 hour period ending at 1300 PST on Nov. 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large crash occurred, involving at least 65 vehicles with one fatality, due to dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed early in the morning, with an inversion in place and enhanced by wildfire smoke in the area. Around 60 to 65 vehicles piled up on I-5 southbound, roughly 20 miles north of Eugene, near Harrisburg, in Linn County. At least one confirmed dead, and four others taken to the hospital. I-5 was closed for many hours. Six of the vehicles leaked fluids on the roadway triggering a HAZMAT response.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado occurred in Cherryville with minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado occurred on Saturday evening northeast of Cherryville, OR. Video confirmation was obtained, as well as a storm damage survey.  It did cause damage to trees, powerlines, a vehicle, one residence, and small outbuildings. Estimated wind speeds of 75 to 85 mph which rates this tornado as EF-0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek Fire continued to burn through the month. It grew to approximately 127,283 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek Fire continued to burn through the month, growing to approximately 127,283 acres with 60 percent containment by October 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple RAWS stations across the zone recorded consecutive maximum daily high temperatures of 95 degrees or greater from July 26th through the 31st.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough slid down the coast of British Columbia facilitating a period of strong southerly winds along Blue Mountains foothills, and heavy snow across the mountain areas between November 29th and December 1st. Wind gusts of 60-65mph were measured along the foothills of the northern Blue Mountains and snow accumulations of up to 22 inches were reported in the Washington Cascades, and up to 24 inches in portions of the northern Blue Mountains of Oregon and Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station AS881 measured a gust of 66mph at 0200PDT on November 30th and the Mesonet station OD184 measured a gust of 65mph at 0537PDT on November 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong east wind event brought critical fire weather conditions and above normal temperatures to western Oregon. High pressure developed over eastern Washington and Oregon, spilling across the Cascades to the lowlands of western Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire started on the afternoon of the 9th and was extinguished by the afternoon of the 10th. In all 30 acres were burned, 15 of which was in the park itself. One home and a barn were destroyed.  Around 550 homes near the park were evacuated.  No injuries were reported.  Oregon Department of Forestry and Estacada Fire Department has not determined the cause of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure developed over eastern Washington and Oregon, spilling across the Cascades to the lowlands of western Oregon. As a result, strong east winds on Friday brought critical fire weather conditions and above normal temperatures across much of western Oregon. Winds gusted between 20 and 30 mph on Friday, and eased by Saturday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vitae Springs Fire started in South Salem Friday evening and was fully contained by late Saturday morning.  The estimated burned area is around 165 acres, mostly in dense brush and wooded areas, including in steep terrain. Approximately 119 single-family homes were in the Level 3 (Go Now) evacuation zone. There were no reported injuries or structure losses due to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek Fire, started August 1st, 2022, continued to burn through the month, growing to approximately 120,700 acres by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek Fire continued to burn through the month, growing to approximately 120,700 acres with 27 percent containment at the end of the month.  The fire grew considerably on the 9th-10th when dry east winds hit the area. On the 9th, Level 3 (Go Now) evacuation notices were posted for the Oakridge, Westfir, and High Prairie communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of surface frontal boundaries, warm air overrunning low level and valley cold pools, and a moderate transport of moisture in a west to southwest flow aloft resulted in impactful wintry conditions across southeast Washington, northeast and central Oregon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 5 miles south southwest of Chenoweth, OR measured 0.25 inches of new ice accumulations from freezing rain at 0715 PST on Dec. 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Winds. A strong frontal system probably. South winds probably.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stations along the coast measured sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts of 60 to 80 mph. Were there any impacts. Any power outages or trees fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Winds. A strong frontal system probably. South winds probably.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The maximum wind recorded was 32 mph with a gust of 59 mph. The high wind caused a tree to fall on a truck on US Highway 26 at 1130 PST on the 27th, killing 3 people and injuring one. The Highway 26 was then closed for most of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Winds. A strong frontal system probably. South winds probably.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusts of 40 to 55 mph throughout the north Willamette Valley.  Gusts came and went through the period, with most of the damage occurring around 1200 PST on the 27th. | |Many trees were uprooted, snapped, or had broken limbs due to the winds.  Several in Garden Home had downed trees, some as large as 2 ft in diameter. PGE and Pacific Power had combined outages of over 100,000 across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Winds. A strong frontal system probably. South winds probably.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusts of 40 to 55 mph throughout the central Willamette Valley.  Gusts came and went through the period, with most of the damage occurring around 1200 PST on the 27th.  ||Many trees were uprooted, snapped, or had broken limbs due to the winds. PGE and Pacific Power combined for outages of over 100,000 across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and ice to the Columbia River Gorge and Upper Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought around 4 to 10 inches of snow through the central gorge, including the Hood River area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought 3 to 5 inches of snow to the Hood River area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unwarned winter storm brought 3 to 5 inches of snow to the Hood River area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and ice to the Columbia River Gorge and Upper Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 7 inches of snow was measured by the public during this winter storm event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and ice to the Columbia River Gorge and Upper Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of snow and ice to the western Columbia River Gorge. Around 5 inches of snow was reported near Multnomah Falls, while 0.5 inch of ice was reported near Corbett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and ice in the Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Underwood CoCoRaHS reported about 0.7 inch ice.  An observer said it was the most treacherous conditions they have seen at their house in terms of traction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 0.2 to 0.3 inch of ice across Eugene, Albany and Corvallis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported 0.25 inch ice in the La Center, WA area. Public reported 0.2 inch ice in the Kalama, WA area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front over western Washington combined with a moist systems arriving off the Pacific Ocean gave rise to heavy snow in the south Washington Cascades, and eventually in the lower elevations.  There was one traffic fatality in the Western Columbia River on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple vehicle accidents on I-84 in the Columbia River Gorge resulted in one fatality on the eastbound lane near Rooster Rock State Park. The accidents were attributed to black ice and strong east winds.  Light rain fell the evening prior, with freezing temperature following late that evening. There may have been a period of freezing rain as well.
